research_gaps: Exact destination of 5.38M TAO mined during Kusanagi/Nakamoto era (Jan 2021 - Oct 2023) is undocumented publicly
No public dashboard for active compute node count across subnets -- 8,000+ figure from aggregator, not on-chain verified
Polychain Capital's 2019 'incubation' terms never disclosed -- unclear if financial support, mining access, or advisory
Total developer count across all 128+ subnets not centrally tracked
Staking APY varies significantly by subnet and changes continuously -- no fixed figure
Opentensor Foundation financials not publicly disclosed beyond estimated $1.8M annual revenue
Protection Fund or Treasury management not documented
Matthew McAteer (third whitepaper co-author) has no public presence in the project post-publication
Exact validator count across all subnets not reported in aggregate
Real yield after inflation not calculable without subnet-specific data
Insider lawsuit (JustM2J LLC v Brewer et al) outcome pending -- proceeding to discovery as of Nov 2025
OTF's timeline for transitioning from Proof of Authority to Proof of Stake not published

identity.name: Bittensor
identity.one_liner: An incentivised network of AI subnets competing to produce digital commodities, funded by Bitcoin-modelled token emissions.

founding_team.name: Jacob Robert Steeves
founding_team.background: BASc Mathematics & Computer Science, Simon Fraser University. ML Researcher at Knowm Inc (DARPA contractor). Software Engineer at Google (2016-2018), reportedly on the Brain team. Co-founded Bittensor in 2016, went full-time in 2018.

founding_team.name: Ala Shaabana
founding_team.role: Co-Founder (now Board, shifted to Crucible Labs Nov 2024)
founding_team.background: BSc Computer Science, University of Windsor. PhD Computer Science (applied AI), McMaster University (2013-2017). VMware R&D, Senior Software Engineer at Instacart, Assistant Professor U of T, Postdoc U of Waterloo. Co-founded Crucible Labs with David Lawee (ex-Alphabet/CapitalG) in Nov 2024.

notable_backers: Digital Currency Group (~500K TAO / ~$175M, launched Yuma subsidiary Nov 2024)
dao5 (~$50M in TAO, acquired 3rd largest validator Dec 2024)
Grayscale (GTAO trust, filed for spot ETF Dec 2025)
OSS Capital / Joseph Jacks ($25M committed to ecosystem)
Stillcore Capital / Jason Calacanis (subnet token fund)
David Lawee (ex-Alphabet CMO / CapitalG founder, co-founded Crucible Labs)

technical.architecture_summary: Bittensor is a Substrate-based Layer 1 blockchain (Subtensor) that coordinates a network of 128+ AI subnets. Each subnet is a competitive marketplace where miners produce AI work (inference, training, prediction, data) and validators score quality using Yuma Consensus. The chain currently runs Proof of Authority (Aura + GRANDPA) with block production controlled by the Opentensor Foundation. Dynamic TAO (launched Feb 2025) introduced per-subnet alpha tokens traded via on-chain AMMs, replacing the previous Root Network validator oligarchy for emission allocation. Block time is 12 seconds with a 360-block tempo (~72 min) for consensus and emission distribution cycles.
technical.consensus_mechanism: Proof of Authority (Aura + GRANDPA) for block production (OTF-controlled). Yuma Consensus for emission distribution within subnets. dTAO market mechanism for cross-subnet emission allocation.
technical.what_is_onchain: TAO transfers, staking/delegation, subnet registration, neuron registration, weight submissions, Yuma Consensus scoring, alpha token AMMs, emission distribution, EVM smart contracts
technical.what_is_offchain: AI inference and model execution (miners), quality scoring computation (validators), subnet incentive mechanism logic, model storage and training data
key_dependencies: PyPI (Python package distribution -- attack vector in July 2024)

security_incidents.description: Wallet drain: single user lost 28,000+ TAO ($11.2M). Part of coordinated insider attack. Attacker split funds across 18 wallets, bridged to Ethereum.

PyPI supply chain attack: malicious Bittensor v6.12.2 package uploaded to PyPI, exfiltrated coldkey private keys. ~32,000 TAO (~$8M) stolen. OTF halted the network in safe mode. Lawsuit filed Jan 2025 naming former OTF employees Ayden Brewer, Jon Litz, and Jason St. George. Total combined theft ~61,794 TAO (~$30.3M).
Separate typosquatting campaign: 5 malicious PyPI packages with similar names (bitensor, bittenso etc) hijacked stake_extrinsic function. Discovered by GitLab, removed within 6 hours.

tokenomics.token_standard: Native coin (Substrate-based L1). Wrapped version (wTAO) on Ethereum: 0x77e06c9eccf2e797fd462a92b6d7642ef85b0a44
token_utility: Staking to validators and subnets (earn emissions)
dTAO subnet investment (TAO swapped for alpha tokens via AMM)

initial_allocation.notes: All TAO distributed through mining/validating. No ICO, no pre-mine, no VC allocation. However, 5.38M TAO mined Jan 2021 - Oct 2023 with very limited participants. Destination of early-mined TAO is undocumented.

distribution.distribution_method: Fair launch (mining). Network went live Jan 3, 2021 (Kusanagi). All tokens earned through network participation. No token sale, no pre-mine. Polychain Capital incubated in 2019; DCG and dao5 accumulated through early mining or OTC. Largest single wallet holds ~1.55M TAO (~20% of supply at time of analysis).

emissions.halving_schedule: Supply-milestone-based (not block-height). Halves when 50% of remaining unmined supply is emitted. First halving at 10.5M TAO, second at 15.75M, third at 18.375M.
emissions.burn_mechanism: Recycling: TAO spent on subnet registration, miner registration, and transaction fees is removed from circulation and returned to the unissued emission pool. Does not reduce max supply but delays halvings and reduces circulating supply.

staking.staking_type: Delegated Proof of Stake (nominator staking). Post-dTAO: stake TAO into specific subnets (converted to alpha tokens) or stake on Root (TAO-to-TAO).
staking.lock_up_period: No hard lock-up. Unstaking involves alpha-to-TAO conversion at current AMM rate (price impact risk on large positions).
staking_risks: Alpha token price risk (subnet tokens can lose value vs TAO)
Validator take rate (0-18% of delegator rewards)
Coldkey exposure on every stake/unstake operation
Concentration risk: top 1% of wallets control ~90% of stake
staking.slashing_conditions: No formal slashing mechanism documented. Validators who deviate from Yuma Consensus build weaker bonds and earn less, but are not slashed.

tokenomics.token_model_classification: Bitcoin-modelled disinflationary with Darwinian AI incentive layer
tokenomics.tokenomics_notes: TAO deliberately mirrors Bitcoin: 21M cap, halving schedule, PoW-analogous distribution. Key difference: halvings are supply-milestone-based (not block-height), and recycled TAO delays halving dates. The 41/41/18 emission split (miners/validators/subnet owner) is fixed. dTAO introduced per-subnet alpha tokens with their own 21M supply caps, creating a two-tier token economy. Root staking yields ~14-20% nominal APY; high-performing subnets can yield 80%+ but with significant alpha price risk.

methods.description: Delegate TAO to validators on specific subnets or on the Root Network. Subnet staking converts TAO to alpha tokens; Root staking keeps TAO-to-TAO. Earn emissions proportional to stake.
methods.estimated_returns: Root: ~14-20% APY nominal. Subnets: 80%+ possible but with alpha price risk.
barriers_to_entry: Need to research subnets before staking
Alpha token price risk on subnet staking

methods.description: Run AI models on a specific subnet, competing with other miners for quality-based emissions. Requires GPU hardware and subnet-specific software setup.
methods.minimum_hardware: NVIDIA RTX 4090 minimum (A100/H100 for competitive subnets), 256GB RAM, NVMe SSD, 10Gbps bandwidth
methods.estimated_returns: Highly variable by subnet and competitive position. Top miners earn disproportionately.

methods.description: Run a validator on one or more subnets. Score miner outputs, distribute emissions, earn validator rewards. Requires staking TAO and running infrastructure.
methods.minimum_hardware: High-end GPU (A100/H100), 256GB+ RAM, NVMe SSD, 10Gbps bandwidth
methods.estimated_returns: 41% of subnet emissions split among validators by stake weight, minus take rate shared with delegators.
barriers_to_entry: Minimum 1,000 TAO stake required per subnet

methods.description: Create a new subnet for an AI workload. Define the incentive mechanism, attract miners and validators. Earn 18% of subnet emissions as owner.
methods.minimum_hardware: Development environment plus infrastructure for incentive mechanism
methods.estimated_returns: 18% of subnet emissions. Top subnets earn >$200K/day at current emission rates and TAO price.
barriers_to_entry: Dynamic TAO burn cost for subnet registration (fluctuates significantly)
No formal grants programme -- protocol emissions are the funding mechanism

developer_resources.developer_community_size: 171 contributors on core repo; total across 128+ subnets substantially larger but not tracked

usage_metrics.compute_delivered: 128 active subnets covering inference, training, prediction, data, storage, and more. 8,000+ GPU nodes reported.
usage_metrics.revenue_source: Protocol emissions (no direct protocol revenue from external demand yet)
usage_metrics.usage_notes: 102,000+ wallet addresses. Daily emissions of 3,600 TAO (~$640K/day at current price) distributed across subnets. Chutes (SN64) is the largest subnet by emission share (~14.4%). Real external demand vs emission-driven activity is difficult to distinguish. No public metrics for inference volume, latency, or throughput at the network level.

community.governance_participation_rate: Not measurable -- Senate is a small fixed group (top validators by stake), Triumvirate is 3 OTF employees
community.governance_model: Bicameral: Triumvirate (3 OTF employees propose changes) + Senate (top validators by delegated stake vote, 50%+1 required). dTAO adds market-driven economic governance via subnet staking flows.
key_community_channels: Taostats.io -- real-time subnet and validator analytics
Novelty Search podcast (YouTube) -- official OTF podcast
community.sentiment_summary: Technically sophisticated community with strong conviction. 'Bitcoin of AI' narrative drives long-term holders. Institutional interest (Grayscale ETF, DCG/Yuma) validates thesis. Key concerns: dTAO gaming, meme coin subnets, OTF centralisation, post-halving price decline. Community graded Bittensor 'B' for 2025. Culture described as 'professional, not crypto-native' which is refreshing but can feel closed.
community.community_notes: X follower count split across @opentensor (official) and @bittensor (~8,774) accounts. Telegram is community-run, not listed as official channel. Notable community figures: @const_reborn, @markjeffrey, @Old_Samster, @brodydotai. Institutional ecosystem includes Yuma (DCG), Crucible Labs (Shaabana/Lawee), Latent Holdings (Joseph Jacks).

infrastructure_decentralisation.evidence: 128 active subnets with permissionless participation. 8,000+ GPU nodes across the network. Miners and validators operate independently. However, Subtensor blockchain runs Proof of Authority with block production controlled entirely by the Opentensor Foundation. OTF can unilaterally halt the network (demonstrated July 2024). Transition to PoS planned but no timeline published. Standalone L1 with no shared security from external chain.

governance_decentralisation.evidence: Bicameral governance: Triumvirate (3 OTF employees) proposes all changes; Senate (top validators by stake) votes. 50%+1 required. dTAO (Feb 2025) meaningfully shifted emission allocation from validator oligarchy to market mechanism. However, OTF still controls block production and governance proposals. SN28 intervention (OTF used root stake to crash a meme coin subnet 98%) demonstrated centralised override capability. Academic analysis shows top 1% of wallets control ~90% of stake (Gini ~0.98).

token_distribution_fairness.evidence: Technically a fair launch: no ICO, no pre-mine, no VC allocation, no token sale. All TAO earned through mining. However, 5.38M TAO mined during 2021-2023 with very limited participants and no public accounting of destination. Polychain 'incubated' in 2019, DCG 'invested' in 2021 -- likely through early mining access. Largest single wallet holds ~1.55M TAO (~20% of supply). Staking rate 70-90% means extremely thin free float. Fair launch label is technically accurate but practically the distribution resembles insider accumulation.

censorship_resistance.evidence: Permissionless subnet creation and miner registration (with TAO burn). No content policy on individual subnets. However, OTF validated all blocks and can censor transactions at the chain level. Network halt in July 2024 and SN28 intervention demonstrate that OTF can and will intervene. TAO burn barrier for registration creates capital-gated access.

data_sovereignty.evidence: Miners run models locally on their own hardware. Validators score independently. Self-custodial wallets (coldkey/hotkey architecture). No platform data collection or surveillance. However, coldkey must be decrypted for every stake/unstake operation, creating repeated exposure risk (exploited in 2024 hack). No ZK or encryption on the inference layer.

open_source_transparency.evidence: All core code is open source (MIT license) across 64 public repos. Active development with frequent commits. However: no third-party security audit has been publicly disclosed despite multi-billion dollar market cap. No SECURITY.md file on main repo. No formal bug bounty programme. Early token distribution (5.38M TAO) is opaque. OTF financials not publicly reported.

freedom_score.freedom_summary: Bittensor scores a C, reflecting genuine decentralisation at the compute/subnet layer undermined by significant centralisation at the chain and governance layer. The subnet architecture is innovative and permissionless with 128+ independent markets. However, the Opentensor Foundation validates all blocks (Proof of Authority), can halt the network unilaterally, and controls governance proposals through a 3-person Triumvirate. The 'fair launch' is technically clean but practically resulted in concentrated distribution (top 1% hold ~90% of stake). No security audit, a $28-30M insider hack, and no bug bounty programme are serious concerns for a protocol of this scale.

what_works_today: Largest decentralised AI network by market cap and activity -- 128 subnets, 8,000+ GPU nodes
Bitcoin-modelled tokenomics with genuine fair launch (no ICO, no pre-mine, no VC allocation)
dTAO (Feb 2025) replaced validator oligarchy with market-driven emission allocation
Real institutional adoption: Grayscale Trust/ETF filing, DCG subsidiary (Yuma), Binance/Coinbase/Kraken listings
Subnet architecture allows permissionless creation of AI marketplaces for any workload
First halving completed (Dec 2025) -- disinflationary pressure now active
Leading subnets producing real outputs: Chutes (inference, billions of tokens/day on OpenRouter), Nineteen (inference speed records), Gradients (model training cheaper than Google Cloud)
what_is_hype: 'Decentralised' claim undermined by OTF controlling all block production (Proof of Authority)
Fair launch narrative ignores 5.38M TAO accumulated by limited participants pre-subnet era
'8,000+ GPU nodes' -- not independently verifiable, no public dashboard
Miner quality claims -- academic analysis shows performance-to-reward correlation only 0.10-0.30; stake weight drives rewards, not AI output quality
Most subnets have not demonstrated output quality exceeding centralised alternatives
dTAO susceptible to gaming (SN28 meme coin subnet) and speculation over genuine utility signals
'102,000+ wallet addresses' likely includes inactive and duplicate wallets
key_risks: OTF validates ALL blocks (PoA) and can halt the network -- single most critical centralisation vector
$28-30M insider hack by former employees -- lawsuit pending, demonstrates personnel/supply chain risk
No third-party security audit disclosed for core protocol
Top 1% of wallets control ~90% of stake (Gini ~0.98) -- extreme concentration
Post-halving inflation still ~12% annually -- if external demand doesn't materialise, sell pressure persists
Ala Shaabana (co-founder) partially departed to Crucible Labs -- key person risk on Jacob Steeves
Regulatory classification uncertain -- alpha tokens add further complexity
Competition from centralised AI providers remains orders of magnitude ahead on UX and capability
PyPI supply chain dependency exploited twice -- fundamental tooling vulnerability
competitive_advantages: Largest DeAI network by market cap and subnet count
Bitcoin-modelled tokenomics provide clear, credible scarcity narrative
Institutional adoption (Grayscale, DCG/Yuma, Bitwise) creates legitimacy moat
Subnet architecture is genuinely innovative -- permissionless AI marketplace creation
Strong technical community with high development velocity
competitive_disadvantages: Chain layer is centralised (PoA) -- behind peers like Bittensor's own marketing suggests
No security audit -- behind Morpheus (4 audits) and other DeAI projects
UX friction: Bittensor wallet setup, bridging from EVM/Solana, complex staking mechanics
Isolated ecosystem -- not natively interoperable with EVM or Solana chains
Incentive gaming (relay mining, bucket copying) remains an ongoing challenge
comparable_projects: Morpheus (MOR) -- decentralised AI agent infrastructure with fair launch
Akash Network (AKT) -- decentralised cloud compute marketplace
SingularityNET / ASI Alliance (FET/AGIX/OCEAN) -- AI agent marketplace
assessment.outlook: Bittensor occupies a unique position as the largest DeAI network with genuine institutional backing and a Bitcoin-modelled scarcity narrative. The next 12 months will determine whether dTAO-driven market allocation produces real quality differentiation or degenerates into speculation. The transition from Proof of Authority to Proof of Stake is the critical infrastructure milestone. If external demand for subnet services materialises at scale, the tokenomics become extremely compelling post-halving. If not, the network risks becoming an expensive incentive game with limited real-world utility.
assessment.overall_assessment: Bittensor is the most ambitious and well-capitalised DeAI project, with a genuinely innovative subnet architecture and institutional adoption that no competitor matches. However, the gap between its decentralisation narrative and operational reality is significant. OTF controls block production, can halt the network, and retains governance proposal power. The 'fair launch' produced a practically concentrated distribution. No security audit for a $2B+ protocol is indefensible. The $28M insider hack exposed real operational failures. For practitioners: TAO staking offers meaningful yield with institutional-grade exchange access, but understand that you are trusting the Opentensor Foundation more than the marketing suggests.
